title: Refund-Cycling Volume Inflation via Sequential Purchase-Return Loops
narrative: Fraudster initiates 12-18 legitimate-appearing purchases of $47-$89 each within a 90-minute window, then immediately requests refunds on 8-10 of those transactions before submitting a new wave of purchases using the same card and device. The pattern repeats 3-4 times across 6-8 hours, creating artificial transaction volume to age the account and evade new-card velocity filters. Each cycle increases TransactionAmt slightly ($47â†’$63â†’$81) to test limits. All transactions share identical addr1, dist1 under 2km, and P_emaildomain matching a free provider. The final cycle typically includes one high-value purchase ($340-$580) immediately after the last refund batch clears, exploiting the inflated card1_txn_count_24hr (38-52 transactions) to appear as an established customer. Refund requests occur 8-15 minutes post-purchase, never triggering chargeback workflows but creating processing confusion.
indicators: 3-4 distinct purchase waves separated by 85-140 minute gaps, each preceded by refund cluster
TransactionAmt progression within session: incremental increases of $14-$22 per wave until final spike
Refund timing: 85% of refunds occur 8-15 minutes after original purchase timestamp
card1_amt_zscore exceeds 2.8 for final transaction despite high txn_count suggesting normalization
dist1 consistency under 2km across all transactions with identical addr1 and DeviceInfo
P_emaildomain matches R_emaildomain on 100% of refund requests, both free providers (gmail/yahoo)
summary: The same card makes 38-52 purchases and refunds in 24 hours, organized into 3-4 waves separated by 85-140 minute gaps, with refund requests occurring 8-15 minutes after each purchase and transaction amounts climbing from $47 to over $340. All transactions share the same address, device, and location within 2km, with matching free email domains for both purchase and refund requests.
reasoning: The attacker artificially inflates transaction history to make the card look established and bypass new-account velocity checks, using rapid purchase-refund cycles to age the account before attempting a high-value fraudulent purchase that exploits the inflated transaction count to appear legitimate.
indicator_explanations: Card shows dozens of purchases with nearly half immediately refunded same day
Activity comes in distinct bursts separated by consistent cooling-off periods
Purchase amounts creep upward across waves then suddenly jump at finale
Refunds arrive suspiciously fast after purchases in tight time windows
Final transaction amount defies the card's established normal spending despite volume
All activity pinned to identical location and device throughout the scheme
Purchaser and refund requester emails always match using free provider accounts
